food_subgroup: Grilled paneer dish; marinated fresh cheese grilled in tandoor

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for paneer as a food ingredient (FOODON_03302114). No exact entry for paneer tikka as a prepared dish exists; closest match used at ingredient level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP corresponds to paneer as a dairy product. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for paneer tikka as a composite prepared dish; ingredient-level anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for paneer tikka or paneer as a prepared dish. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of paneer tikka is approximately 200 g, comprising 4–6 cubes of marinated grilled paneer, often served with mint chutney and sliced onion.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer Tikka — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), composite dish values; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cottage cheese and spice marinade components for cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides nutrient data for paneer (fresh cottage cheese) and for tandoori-style preparations. Paneer tikka values are derived from the ICMR-NIN paneer entry (per 100 g: energy 265 kcal, protein 18.3 g, fat 20.8 g, carbohydrate 1.2 g) adjusted for the marinade contribution (yogurt, spices, mustard, oil) based on standard recipe proportions reported in ICMR-NIN composite dish methodology. The marinade adds approximately 10–15% by weight and modifies energy, fat, and carbohydrate slightly. Final per-100 g values represent the cooked, grilled composite dish. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01015, Cheese, cottage, creamed) used for cross-validation of protein and fat ranges.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for paneer tikka as a composite dish. Paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) is a high-protein, high-fat, very low-carbohydrate food. The University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021) list full-fat dairy products and fresh cheese analogues in the GI range of 15–30. A GI of 27 is assigned by analogy with full-fat cottage cheese and fresh cheese entries in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (27 × 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2) / 100 = 2.4, rounded to 2.0 given estimation uncertainty. The very low carbohydrate content (4.5 g per 100 g) means GL is negligible regardless of exact GI value.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade and seasoning (~320 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of portion size, as a 200 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 640 mg sodium (~28% of the 2300 mg daily limit); requesting reduced salt in the marinade and limiting accompanying chutneys can help manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (4.5 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~27)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer tikka is a low-GI, high-protein food with minimal carbohydrate load, making it a suitable protein choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high saturated fat content warrants attention in the context of overall dietary fat management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~9.8 g per 100 g) from full-fat paneer
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of paneer tikka is clinically relevant for cardiovascular disease risk management; individuals should limit portion size to 100–150 g and balance this dish with high-fibre vegetables and whole grains to mitigate LDL-raising effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer tikka is a popular North Indian appetiser and main course dish made from cubes of fresh paneer (Indian cottage cheese) that are marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture — typically including mustard, turmeric, cumin, coriander, chilli, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice — and then grilled at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The result is a dish with a lightly charred, smoky exterior and a soft, creamy interior. Paneer tikka is entirely vegetarian and gluten-free, making it one of the most widely ordered vegetarian dishes at North Indian restaurants globally, including in Singapore where it is a staple on menus at restaurants such as Curry Village, Mirch Masalah, Bombay Curry and Kebabs, Aryana, and Khushboo. Nutritionally, paneer tikka is high in protein (approximately 16.5 g per 100 g) and calcium (approximately 480 mg per 100 g), reflecting the dairy base of paneer. It is also relatively high in fat (approximately 17 g per 100 g), predominantly saturated fat from the full-fat cheese. Carbohydrate content is very low (approximately 4.5 g per 100 g), giving the dish a low glycemic index of approximately 27 and a negligible glycemic load per serving. This nutritional profile makes paneer tikka a suitable high-protein, low-carbohydrate option for individuals managing blood glucose, though the saturated fat content is a consideration for cardiovascular health. Sodium content from the marinade and seasoning is moderate (~320 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is typically served with mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges as a starter or as part of a larger Indian meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in paneer tikka?
Is paneer tikka good for diabetics?
What is paneer tikka made of?
Is paneer tikka high in fat?
Paneer tikka vs chicken tikka nutrition
meal_context: field survey data Indian restaurant menu item

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN (2017) provides direct nutrient data for paneer as a primary ingredient and for tandoori-style preparations. No single ICMR-NIN entry for paneer tikka as a composite dish exists; values are adapted from the paneer base entry with marinade adjustment per ICMR-NIN composite dish methodology. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database dairy/fresh cheese category. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Food name corrected from 'Paaneer Tikka' (input spelling) to canonical English 'Paneer Tikka'. Both spellings are preserved in aliases. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) paneer base data with marinade contribution estimated per standard recipe proportions; values represent the cooked, grilled composite dish per 100 g. 3. GI of 27 is an analogous estimate based on full-fat dairy/fresh cheese entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) International GI Tables; no direct GI measurement for paneer tikka was found in approved sources. 4. Serving size of 200 g is a representative restaurant portion based on typical North Indian restaurant servings in Singapore; actual portion sizes vary by restaurant. 5. Halal status is listed as true as paneer is a dairy product with no meat components; individual restaurant halal certification should be verified independently. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are matched at the paneer ingredient level as no composite dish codes for paneer tikka exist in these ontologies. 7. Price range SGD 15.90–18.90 from field survey data input data is noted but not included in the nutritional record as it is not a nutritional field.
